title: Hardware-Rooted AI Security That Won’t Slow You Down | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA argues its Blackwell-embedded Confidential Computing protects data and model weights during inference at up to 98% of non-secured performance (typically under 8% overhead), benchmarked on an HGX B300 running a 397B Qwen MoE model, positioning secure inference as production-ready and removing a key enterprise adoption blocker.

insights.claim: NVIDIA Confidential Computing delivers inference at up to 98% of the performance of solutions that don't enable CC, with throughput and per-token latency overhead typically under 8% across concurrency, batch sizes, and token lengths.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: The near-zero overhead claim is the crux that could turn confidential computing from a niche compliance feature into a default deployment mode.
insights.quote: NVIDIA Confidential Computing delivers security at 98% of performance of solutions that don’t enable CC
insights.locator: Hardware-Rooted AI Security That Won’t Slow You Down

insights.claim: On an HGX B300 with Blackwell Ultra running Qwen 3.5-397B-A17B at FP8, CC throughput/GPU degradation stayed within roughly 1-7.5% and TPOT within about 0.9-8.1% versus CC off across concurrency levels from 4 to 256.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Benchmarked on a frontier-scale MoE model, the tight overhead band is the concrete evidence enterprises will scrutinize before adopting secure inference.

insights.claim: CC performance impact on Blackwell comes from just two sources: secure work-submission latency from encryption and kernel launches (worse for small units of work) and reduced encrypted host-to-device CPU-to-GPU bandwidth.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Identifies exactly where CC taxes performance, so workload architects can predict which inference patterns pay the most overhead.
insights.quote: Increasing the amount of work performed per GPU work launch reduces the impact of the secure launch overhead.
insights.locator: Optimizing AI inference performance in Confidential Computing

themes.theme: Hardware root of trust and attestation

insights.claim: Every Blackwell GPU (RTX PRO 6000, HGX B200, HGX B300) maintains a private signing key fused at manufacturing that is never exposed to software, firmware, or the host system, forming the foundation of the attestation chain.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Silicon-fused keys make CC a hardware moat competitors can't replicate in software, reinforcing Blackwell's enterprise value beyond raw FLOPs.
insights.quote: the GPU maintains a private signing key that is fused at the time of manufacturing and never exposed to software, firmware, or the host system

insights.claim: HGX B200 and HGX B300 support confidential computing across up to 8 GPUs using NVLink encryption, extending trusted execution beyond a single accelerator.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Multi-GPU confidential compute via encrypted NVLink is required to secure large models that span accelerators, a differentiator for frontier-scale secure inference.
insights.quote: support confidential computing across multiple GPUs (up to 8) with NVIDIA NVLink encryption

insights.claim: Remote attestation via NRAS verifies a signed evidence bundle (GPU hardware report plus CPU TEE measurements from AMD SEV-SNP or Intel TDX) against a reference integrity manifest, and because it is a one-time startup handshake it adds no per-request inference latency.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: One-time attestation means the security handshake doesn't tax steady-state inference, key to the 'won't slow you down' claim.
insights.quote: The attestation handshake is typically a one-time startup event. Once the workload is running, attestation does not add latency to individual inference requests.

insights.claim: Three specific software fixes mitigate CC overhead: FlashInfer swaps event timers for the GPU global timer register so autotuners stay accurate in CC mode, SGLang adds an async D2H copy worker to restore compute/copy overlap, and SGLang adds piecewise CUDA graph replay to cut kernel-launch overhead amplified under CC.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: The near-native performance depends on upstream open-source framework work, not just silicon, showing CC readiness is a full-stack effort.
insights.quote: CC can otherwise make many host-to-device and device-to-host copies effectively synchronous during cudaMemcpyAsync
insights.locator: Optimizing AI inference performance in Confidential Computing

insights.claim: NVIDIA is upstreaming CC optimizations into community inference frameworks (specific PRs 28251 for SGLang and 3638 for FlashInfer), signaling a strategy to make confidential inference work across the open ecosystem rather than only proprietary tooling.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Upstreaming lowers adoption friction and embeds NVIDIA's secure-compute assumptions into widely used inference engines.
insights.quote: NVIDIA continues to work with upstream communities for inference frameworks to ensure these frameworks are optimized for performance.

insights.claim: NVIDIA frames Confidential Computing as purpose-built for the agentic AI era, protecting enterprise data and proprietary model weights during active inference so organizations can meet regulations like HIPAA, GDPR Article 32, and NIST zero-trust while scaling any model.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: Targets regulated verticals (healthcare, finance, sovereign) where data-in-use protection is the gating requirement for AI deployment.
insights.quote: CC enables the protection of enterprise data and proprietary model weights and the model itself during active inference.
insights.locator: AI has transformed how organizations operate

insights.claim: A lead hardware architect states the goal is for confidential computing to become the default mode of GPU deployment, indicating NVIDIA intends CC to be standard rather than an opt-in premium feature.
insights.speaker: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
insights.so_what: If CC becomes default, secure inference stops being a differentiated SKU and becomes table stakes, reshaping how competitors must respond.
insights.quote: She is passionate about confidential computing as a technology and looks forward to it becoming the default mode of deployment.

source_meta.author: Sheel Pethe, Vidhya Krishnan, Aruna Manjunatha, Matheen Raza and Jamie Li
